18.title: Article 18 Amendment Jeanne Ostroff

18.excerpt: I move that the town vote to amend the main motion by replacing it in its entirety and inserting the following in its place: Town Meeting commemorates the 250th anniversary of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, originally approved on June 20, 1776; And that Town Meeting affirms its support of the Constitution of the United States through the following resolution: Whereas: 1. The Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, enacted June 20, 1776, reflects the courageous spirit and determination of the land’s inhabitants and our ancestors in the pursuit of liberty and justice in the face of tyranny. 2. The principles articulated in the 1776 Resolve resonate deeply in our contemporary society, reminding us of the long-standing importance of local governance and the right of self-determination. 3. The Constitution of the United States serves as a guiding document that enshrines the freedoms fought for by our forebears, ensuring the protection of rights for all citizens. Now, therefore, be it resolved that: 1. We, the inhabitants of Natick, do hereby celebrate the legacy of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence as a testament to our local history and our ongoi
18.floor_impact: May amend the main motion; moderator should review scope and order of consideration.
18.motion_text: I move that the town vote to amend the main motion by replacing it in its entirety and inserting the following in its place: Town Meeting commemorates the 250th anniversary of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, originally approved on June 20, 1776; And that Town Meeting affirms its support of the Constitution of the United States through the following resolution: Whereas: 1. The Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, enacted June 20, 1776, reflects the courageous spirit and determination of the land’s inhabitants and our ancestors in the pursuit of liberty and justice in the face of tyranny. 2. The principles articulated in the 1776 Resolve resonate deeply in our contemporary society, reminding us of the long-standing importance of local governance and the right of self-determination. 3. The Constitution of the United States serves as a guiding document that enshrines the freedoms fought for by our forebears, ensuring the protection of rights for all citizens. Now, therefore, be it resolved that: 1. We, the inhabitants of Natick, do hereby celebrate the legacy of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence as a testament to our local history and our ongoing commitment to liberty. 2. We affirm our support for the Constitution of the United States, and through our oath of office recognize it as a vital framework for justice, equality, and the rule of law. 3. We encourage the citizens of Natick to reflect on the significance of this anniversary and to engage in discussions that promote civic responsibility and historical awareness. 4. We instruct the Town Clerk to place a copy of this resolution, the Natick Town Meeting Resolve of Independence dated June 20, 1776, and the Constitution of the United States in the town records so that such are available for viewing by the wider community to honor this important milestone.
open_items: Confirm parsed motion text against the official PDF.
Determine precedence, scope, and vote threshold before floor use.
18.title: Article 18 Amendment Foss

3.title: Article 3 Procedural Motion Coffey

meeting_level_motions.excerpt: Move that a CONSENT AGENDA shall be in order during the First Session of this Spring Annual Town Meeting; provided, however that the Moderator must ensure that said motion is published in the Finance Committee Recommendation Book which is required to be provided at least seven (7) days prior to the opening of the First Session of this Spring Annual Town Meeting. The Moderator shall propose to Town Meeting Members a “Consent Agenda” in the form of a motion listing all articles and respective motions which (based on the advice of the Finance Committee, Town Clerk and Town Counsel) the Moderator deems to be non-controversial. At said First Session of the meeting, seven (7) Town Meeting Members may be recognized by the Moderator so that any article shall be removed from the Consent Agenda and debated and voted upon in accordance with the provisions of Town Meeting rules and practices. All articles remaining in the Consent Agenda shall then be voted upon by a single majority vote without sponsor presentation or debate.

meeting_level_motions.excerpt: Move that the Town vote to take Articles 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 14, and 15 out of order and that they be “Passed by Consent” in accordance with the Motion published in the Consent Agenda distributed in the Finance Committee Book of Recommendations. ARTICLE 1 – Authorize Select Board to Acquire, Obtain, Abandon or Relocate Easements Motion: MOVE that the Town vote to authorize the Select Board, during Fiscal Year 2027, to acquire on behalf of the Town any and all easements for any of the following purposes: roads, sidewalks,vehicular and/or pedestrian access or passage, drainage and utilities, provided however that such authorization pertains only to easements acquired at no cost to the Town; and, further, to authorize the Select Board, subsequent to a public hearing, during Fiscal Year 2027 to abandon or relocate easements acquired for any of the foregoing purposes. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 2 – Committee Article Motion: MOVE to hear the report of the Natick Net Zero Committee. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 4 – Stabilization Funds & OPEB Motion: Move that the Town vote to appropriate $6,924,290 from FY 2026 Certified Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Town’s Stabi

meeting_level_motions.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to take Articles 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 14, and 15 out of order and that they be “Passed by Consent” in accordance with the Motion published in the Consent Agenda distributed in the Finance Committee Book of Recommendations. ARTICLE 1 – Authorize Select Board to Acquire, Obtain, Abandon or Relocate Easements Motion: MOVE that the Town vote to authorize the Select Board, during Fiscal Year 2027, to acquire on behalf of the Town any and all easements for any of the following purposes: roads, sidewalks,vehicular and/or pedestrian access or passage, drainage and utilities, provided however that such authorization pertains only to easements acquired at no cost to the Town; and, further, to authorize the Select Board, subsequent to a public hearing, during Fiscal Year 2027 to abandon or relocate easements acquired for any of the foregoing purposes. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 2 – Committee Article Motion: MOVE to hear the report of the Natick Net Zero Committee. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 4 – Stabilization Funds & OPEB Motion: Move that the Town vote to appropriate $6,924,290 from FY 2026 Certified Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Town’s Stabilization Funds and OPEB Trust Fund as follows: 1. $5,206,342 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Capital Stabilization Fund established by the vote of Article 2 of the 2010 Fall Annual Town Meeting, as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended. 2. $1,127,170 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the General Stabilization Fund established under Article 22 of the warrant for Annual Town Meeting of 1961, as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended. 1 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION 3. $26,668 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Operational/Rainy Day Stabilization Fund established under Article 4 of the 2011 Spring Annual Town Meeting as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended. 4. $564,110 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the OPEB Trust Fund established under Article 15 of the 2017 Spring Annual Town Meeting, as authorized by Chapter 32B, Section 20 of the General Laws, as amended by Section 15 of Chapter 218 of the Acts of 2016. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 5 – Unpaid Bills Motion: No Action ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 6 – PEG Access and Cable Related Fund Motion: Move that the Town vote to appropriate from the PEG Access and Cable Related Fund, established by vote of the 2019 Special Town Meeting #1, Article 1, as authorized by Chapter 44, Section 53F 3⁄4 of the General Laws, as amended, the sum of $286,649.67 to fund PEG access programming. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 7 – Amend Bylaw Article 41A (Revolving Funds) Move to amend Article 41A (Revolving Funds) of the Natick Town Bylaws as provided for under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 44, section 53E ½, by striking the existing language, and replacing it with the following: Section 1. Purpose This by-law establishes and authorizes revolving funds for use by Town departments, boards, committees, agencies or officers in connection with the operation of programs 2 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION or activities that generate fees, charges or other receipts to support all or some of the expenses of those programs or activities. These revolving funds are established under and governed by General Laws Chapter 44, Section 53E1/2. Section 2. Expenditure Limitations Liabilities may be incurred against and funds may be expended from a revolving fund established and authorized by this by-law without appropriation subject to the following Limitations: a) Fringe benefits of full time employees whose salaries or wages are paid from the fund shall also be paid from the fund; b) No liability shall be incurred in excess of the available balance of the fund; and, c) In any fiscal year, the limit on the amount that may be spent from a revolving fund may be increased with the approval of the Select Board and finance committee. Section 3. Interest Interest earned on the monies credited to a revolving fund established by this by-law shall be credited to the general fund. Section 4. Procedures and Reports Except as provided in General Laws Chapter 44, Section 53E1/2 and this by-law, the laws, charter provisions, by-laws, rules, regulations, policies or procedures that govern the receipt and custody of town’s monies and the expenditure and payment of town funds shall apply to the use of a revolving fund established and authorized by the is by-law. The town comptroller shall include a statement on the collections credited to each fund, the encumbrances and expenditures charged to the fund and the balance available for expenditure in the regular report the town comptroller provides the department, board, committee, agency or officer on appropriations made for its use. Section 5. Authorized Revolving Funds The table establishes: A. Each revolving fund authorized for use by a town department, board, committee, agency or officer, B. The department or agency head, board, committee or officer authorized to spend from each fund, C. The fees, charges and other monies charged and received by the department, board, committee, agency or officer in connection with the program or activity for which the fund is established that shall be credited to each fund by the tow comptroller, 3 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION D. The expenses of the program or activity for which each fund may be used, E. Any restriction or condition on expenditures from each fund, F. Any reporting or other requirements that apply to each fund, and G. The fiscal years each fund shall operate under this by-law. 4 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION ***END OF MOTION*** 5 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION ARTICLE GRADE 1 Adaptive Program Leader Adaptive Program Specialist Assistant Leader (Rec) Assistant Swim Coach Attendant (Rec) Building Monitor I (Rec) Bus Dispatcher Bus Driver Camp Arrowhead Counselor Camp Arrowhead Head Counselor Cart Attendant (Golf) Clerical Assistant Club House Attendant (Golf) GRADE 2 Adaptive Program Assistant Administrative Support * Assistant Director - Camps/Programs (Rec) * Assistant Director (Rec) Bookkeeper Building Monitor II Camp Arrowhead Program Supervisor Camp Director (Rec) Community Garden Coordinator Conservation Agent Election Warden Golf Course Mechanic Head Lifeguard Instructor II GRADE 3 * Adult Contractor Beach Manager Building Inspector Camp Arrowhead Nurse Certified Sports Official * Community Liaison Health Care Supervisor Instructor III * Instructor IV Laborer III Nurse (RN) Per Diem Public Health Nurse 9 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION Club House Supervisor (Golf) Concession Manager * Counselor (Rec) Custodian Deputy Animal Control Officer Election Clerk Election Inspector Equipment Operator (Golf) * Head Counselor (Rec) Instructor Laborer I * Leader/Counselor Certified ( Rec) Library Page (Morse) Parking Clerk Ranger/Starter (Golf) Receptionist * Senior Counselor Certified (Rec) Specialist (Rec) Intern Cooperative Labor II Library Assistant (Bacon) Lifeguard Parking Enforcement Officer Plumbing & Wiring Inspector Police Matron * Program Assistant (CS) * Program Supervisor (Rec) Recycling Attendant School Crossing Guard Social Worker Substitute Library Assistant Swim Coach Transportation Coordinator * Water Safety Instructor (Rec) * NOTE: changes to the pay plan presented at this Town Meeting are denoted with a "*". Asterisk and Strike-through notations will be removed prior to inclusion by reference into Article 24, Section 3, Paragraph 3.10. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 14 – Amend Town Bylaw Article 50, Section 16 (Animal Control) Motion: Move that the Town vote to amend Article 50, Section 16 as follows: ● Add a new sentence to the end of Section 16.7 Fines, that reads: “In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to M.G.L. Chapter 140, Section 145B, subsections a-e, a dog owner that cannot provide a certificate of rabies vaccination for their dog within 24 hours of when asked for by a Police Officer, Animal Control Officer or 10 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION Animal Inspector, said dog shall be considered unvaccinated and the owner shall be subject to the dog unvaccinated for rabies fine noted in this section.” ● In Section 16.7, replace any gender specific pronouns with non-gender specific pronouns; and ● Remove “For the purpose of this section, pet shall be defined as follows” from the first line of Section 16.11; and ● Add a new definition to Section 16.11 for “Vaccinated” to be defined “as described in M.G.L. Chapter 140 s.145B, subsections a-e.” So that new Article 50, Section 16.7 reads as follows: 16.7 Fines - If the investigating officer determines that a violation of this section has occurred, fines may be levied according to the following schedule: First offense $50.00 Second offense $75.00 Third and subsequent offenses $100.00 Unrestrained dog found on school or park property $100.00 Dog unlicensed $100.00 Personal Kennel unlicensed $500.00 Dog unvaccinated for rabies $300.00 Commercial Kennel unlicensed $500.00 In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to Chapter 140, Sections 137 and 138 of the Massachusetts General Laws, a dog owner shall license their dogs in the Town of Natick annually by March 1, and shall pay the required license fee. Any dog owner who fails to do so shall pay to the Town of Natick a fine in the amount of $50.00 per dog in addition to the annual license fee as pursuant to Chapter 140, Section 141 of the Massachusetts General Laws. In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to Chapter 140, Sections 137A of the Massachusetts General Laws, a person maintaining a kennel shall license their kennel annually by March 1st, and shall pay the required license fee. Any kennel owner who fails to do so shall pay to the Town of Natick a fine in the amount of $50.00 in addition to the annual license fee as pursuant to Chapter 140, Section 141 of the Massachusetts General Laws. 11 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to M.G.L. Chapter 140, Section 145B, subsections a-e, a pet owner that cannot provide a certificate of rabies vaccination for their dog within 24 hours of when asked for by a Police Officer, Animal Control Officer or Animal Inspector, said dog shall be considered unvaccinated and the owner shall be subject to the dog unvaccinated for rabies fine noted in this section. And so that Article 50 Section 16.11 reads as follows: 16.11 Definitions: • Pet: any domesticated animal, not including livestock or fowl, which is commonly housed within or upon the owner's or keeper's premises. • Commercial Kennels: Either a “Commercial Boarding or Training Kennel”, or a “Commercial Breeder Kennel” as those terms are described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A; • Personal Kennels: As described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A. • Domestic Charitable Kennels: As described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A. • Vaccinated: As described in M.G.L. Chapter 140 s.145B, subsections a-e. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 15 – Community Preservation Act (CPA) Fund Motion: Move that the Town vote to appropriate five percent of the Community Preservation Fund annual revenues in the amount of $73,750 into the Administrative and Operation account to cover Community Preservation Committee (CPC) administrative expenses, of which a portion will be used for the Community Planner salary (Salary) but not to exceed fifty percent of the Salary or $40,000 whichever is less, for the 2027 Fiscal Year, in accordance with MGL c 44B as recommended by the CPC. ***END OF MOTION*** [end of consent agenda motion] 12

1. Purpose This by-law establishes and authorizes revolving funds for use by Town departments, boards, committees, agencies or officers in connection with the operation of programs 2 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION or activities that generate fees, charges or other receipts to support all or some of the expenses of those programs or activities. These revolving funds are established under and governed by General Laws Chapter 44, Section 53E1/2. Section 2. Expenditure Limitations Liabilities may be incurred against and funds may be expended from a revolving fund established and authorized by this by-law without appropriation subject to the following Limitations: a) Fringe benefits of full time employees whose salaries or wages are paid from the fund shall also be paid from the fund; b) No liability shall be incurred in excess of the available balance of the fund; and, c) In any fiscal year, the limit on the amount that may be spent from a revolving fund may be increased with the approval of the Select Board and finance committee. Section 3. Interest Interest earned on the monies credited to a revolving fund established by this by-law shall be credited to the general fund. Section 4. Procedures and Reports Except as provided in General Laws Chapter 44, Section 53E1/2 and this by-law, the laws, charter provisions, by-laws, rules, regulations, policies or procedures that govern the receipt and custody of town’s monies and the expenditure and payment of town funds shall apply to the use of a revolving fund established and authorized by the is by-law. The town comptroller shall include a statement on the collections credited to each fund, the encumbrances and expenditures charged to the fund and the balance available for expenditure in the regular report the town comptroller provides the department, board, committee, agency or officer on appropriations made for its use. Section 5. Authorized Revolving Funds The table establishes: A. Each revolving fund authorized for use by a town department, board, committee, agency or officer, B. The department or agency head, board, committee or officer authorized to spend from each fund, C. The fees, charges and other monies charged and received by the department, board, committee, agency or officer in connection with the program or activity for which the fund is established that shall be credited to each fund by the tow comptroller, 3 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION D. The expenses of the program or activity for which each fund may be used, E. Any restriction or condition on expenditures from each fund, F. Any reporting or other requirements that apply to each fund, and G. The fiscal years each fund shall operate under this by-law. 4 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION ***END OF MOTION*** 5 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION ARTICLE Asterisk and Strike-through notations will be removed prior to inclusion by reference into Article 24, Section 3, Paragraph 3.10. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 14 – Amend Town Bylaw Article 50, Section 16 (Animal Control) Motion: Move that the Town vote to amend Article 50, Section 16 as follows: ● Add a new sentence to the end of Section 16.7 Fines, that reads: “In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to M.G.L. Chapter 140, Section 145B, subsections a-e, a dog owner that cannot provide a certificate of rabies vaccination for their dog within 24 hours of when asked for by a Police Officer, Animal Control Officer or 10 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION Animal Inspector, said dog shall be considered unvaccinated and the owner shall be subject to the dog unvaccinated for rabies fine noted in this section.” ● In Section 16.7, replace any gender specific pronouns with non-gender specific pronouns; and ● Remove “For the purpose of this section, pet shall be defined as follows” from the first line of Section 16.11; and ● Add a new definition to Section 16.11 for “Vaccinated” to be defined “as described in M.G.L. Chapter 140 s.145B, subsections a-e.” So that new Article 50, Section 16.7 reads as follows: 16.7 Fines - If the investigating officer determines that a violation of this section has occurred, fines may be levied according to the following schedule: First offense $50.00 Second offense $75.00 Third and subsequent offenses $100.00 Unrestrained dog found on school or park property $100.00 Dog unlicensed $100.00 Personal Kennel unlicensed $500.00 Dog unvaccinated for rabies $300.00 Commercial Kennel unlicensed $500.00 In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to Chapter 140, Sections 137 and 138 of the Massachusetts General Laws, a dog owner shall license their dogs in the Town of Natick annually by March 1, and shall pay the required license fee. Any dog owner who fails to do so shall pay to the Town of Natick a fine in the amount of $50.00 per dog in addition to the annual license fee as pursuant to Chapter 140, Section 141 of the Massachusetts General Laws. In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to Chapter 140, Sections 137A of the Massachusetts General Laws, a person maintaining a kennel shall license their kennel annually by March 1st, and shall pay the required license fee. Any kennel owner who fails to do so shall pay to the Town of Natick a fine in the amount of $50.00 in addition to the annual license fee as pursuant to Chapter 140, Section 141 of the Massachusetts General Laws. 11 2026 SPRING ANNUAL TOWN MEETING CONSENT AGENDA MOTION In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to M.G.L. Chapter 140, Section 145B, subsections a-e, a pet owner that cannot provide a certificate of rabies vaccination for their dog within 24 hours of when asked for by a Police Officer, Animal Control Officer or Animal Inspector, said dog shall be considered unvaccinated and the owner shall be subject to the dog unvaccinated for rabies fine noted in this section. And so that Article 50 Section 16.11 reads as follows: 16.11 Definitions: • Pet: any domesticated animal, not including livestock or fowl, which is commonly housed within or upon the owner's or keeper's premises. • Commercial Kennels: Either a “Commercial Boarding or Training Kennel”, or a “Commercial Breeder Kennel” as those terms are described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A; • Personal Kennels: As described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A. • Domestic Charitable Kennels: As described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A. • Vaccinated: As described in M.G.L. Chapter 140 s.145B, subsections a-e. ***END OF MOTION*** ARTICLE 15 – Community Preservation Act (CPA) Fund Motion: Move that the Town vote to appropriate five percent of the Community Preservation Fund annual revenues in the amount of $73,750 into the Administrative and Operation account to cover Community Preservation Committee (CPC) administrative expenses, of which a portion will be used for the Community Planner salary (Salary) but not to exceed fifty percent of the Salary or $40,000 whichever is less, for the 2027 Fiscal Year, in accordance with MGL c 44B as recommended by the CPC. ***END OF MOTION*** [end of consent agenda motion] 12

We, the inhabitants of Natick, do hereby celebrate the legacy of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence as a testament to our local history and our ongoi
motions.floor_impact: May amend the main motion; moderator should review scope and order of consideration.
motions.motion_text: I move that the town vote to amend the main motion by replacing it in its entirety and inserting the following in its place: Town Meeting commemorates the 250th anniversary of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, originally approved on June 20, 1776; And that Town Meeting affirms its support of the Constitution of the United States through the following resolution: Whereas: 1. The Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, enacted June 20, 1776, reflects the courageous spirit and determination of the land’s inhabitants and our ancestors in the pursuit of liberty and justice in the face of tyranny. 2. The principles articulated in the 1776 Resolve resonate deeply in our contemporary society, reminding us of the long-standing importance of local governance and the right of self-determination. 3. The Constitution of the United States serves as a guiding document that enshrines the freedoms fought for by our forebears, ensuring the protection of rights for all citizens. Now, therefore, be it resolved that: 1. We, the inhabitants of Natick, do hereby celebrate the legacy of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence as a testament to our local history and our ongoing commitment to liberty. 2. We affirm our support for the Constitution of the United States, and through our oath of office recognize it as a vital framework for justice, equality, and the rule of law. 3. We encourage the citizens of Natick to reflect on the significance of this anniversary and to engage in discussions that promote civic responsibility and historical awareness. 4. We instruct the Town Clerk to place a copy of this resolution, the Natick Town Meeting Resolve of Independence dated June 20, 1776, and the Constitution of the United States in the town records so that such are available for viewing by the wider community to honor this important milestone.
